decision_notes: Use this family when the main task is to narrow many candidate factors before running an optimization study.
Prefer it before RSM when the team does not yet know which factors deserve the expensive follow-on runs.
Use this family when noise factors and robustness are central, not when the goal is to locate a precise optimum.
Escalate to split-plot or blocking logic when some factors are hard to change in the lab.
Use this family after screening when quadratic behavior or interactions are expected.
Do not label a study as optimization unless the design and analysis can actually support an optimum region or stationary point.

common_objectives: study composition trade-offs under sum-to-one constraints
decision_notes: Use this family when the factors are composition proportions instead of ordinary independent process settings.
Constrained variants are especially useful when binder or additive fractions have lower and upper bounds.

recommended_design_families: orthogonal arrays for initial ranking only
decision_notes: Use DOE to reduce the candidate charging matrix before committing to exhaustive testing.
Treat orthogonal arrays as an initial ranking tool; prefer D-optimal or RSM when interactions or second-order terms matter.

common_objectives: reduce experiment count for model fitting
decision_notes: Use this family when the modeling question is driven by information gain and parameter identifiability rather than by direct cell-performance optimization.
This template is a better fit for ECM or electro-thermal fitting plans than for high-level protocol drafting.

common_objectives: estimate main effects and interactions while controlling nuisance variability
run a factorial study under day, batch, channel, or operator constraints
decision_notes: Use this family when the study is still a classical factorial experiment, but the lab has known nuisance sources such as batches, channels, days, fixtures, or operators.
Prefer ordinary blocking first; only introduce confounding when one block cannot hold a full factorial replicate and higher-order interactions can be sacrificed deliberately.

notes: These DOE templates are still starter assets, but they now include literature-backed decision notes and evidence-card links.
Use the linked evidence cards to explain why one design family is more suitable than another.
